researchFindings.claim: Floods are the most common and costly natural disaster in the United States; where it can rain, it can flood — flood risk does not follow map lines precisely
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Be Prepared for a Flood (FEMA V-1005, July 2024)
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Establishes why this guide matters; informs summary

researchFindings.claim: Find your flood zone using FEMA's Flood Map Service Center (msc.fema.gov) — know whether your property is in a Special Flood Hazard Area

Flood WATCH = flooding is possible, monitor conditions; flood WARNING = flooding is occurring or imminent, take action immediately; flash flood WARNING = life-threatening rapid flooding, move to high ground now
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Critical watch/warning distinction for preparednessTips and redFlags

researchFindings.claim: Plan multiple evacuation routes to higher ground; practice evacuation routes and designate a destination (relative's home, hotel, or official shelter) before a flood occurs

Build an emergency kit including: 1 gallon of water per person per day for at least 3 days, non-perishable food, battery-powered radio, flashlight, first aid kit, medications, and copies of important documents stored in a waterproof container
Just 6 inches of moving water can knock an adult off their feet; 12 inches can sweep away a small vehicle; 2 feet can carry away most vehicles including SUVs
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Be Prepared for a Flood (FEMA V-1005, July 2024)
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core warning — Turn Around Don't Drown

researchFindings.claim: Sign up for your community's warning system — the Emergency Alert System and NOAA Weather Radio provide emergency alerts; many counties have local alert systems
Protect property by moving valuables to higher floors, cleaning drains and gutters, installing check valves in plumbing pipes, and considering a sump pump with battery backup
Standard homeowner's insurance does not cover flood damage — flood insurance through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) typically takes up to 30 days to go into effect after purchase
Never walk, swim, or drive through floodwater — Turn Around, Don't Drown; floodwater may be contaminated with sewage and chemicals and can conceal hazards
In flash flood situations, evacuate before water levels rise and potentially cut off evacuation routes — do not wait for an official order if you feel in danger
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Be Prepared for a Flood (FEMA V-1005, July 2024)

researchFindings.claim: Downed power lines in standing water create an electrocution hazard — never touch or approach them; report to utility company
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Red flag / warning during and after flood

identifiedGaps.area: Specific home-hardening costs and materials (sandbags, flood barriers)
identifiedGaps.impact: Useful but too operational for a preparedness overview guide
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Include as a brief preparedness tip pointing users to their local emergency management agency

recommendation: Write a preparedness_guide for the Floods parentTopic covering: (1) know your flood zone via FEMA Flood Map, (2) watch/warning/flash flood warning distinctions, (3) evacuation routes to pre-identified higher ground, (4) 3-day emergency kit with waterproof document storage, (5) sign up for local emergency alerts, (6) home protection actions (check valves, sump pump, move valuables), (7) flood insurance awareness. Key warnings: Turn Around Don't Drown; evacuate before routes are cut off; downed power lines in water are deadly. All claims sourced by FEMA and Red Cross.
